questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The half-lives of two radioactive nuclides $A$ and $B$ are 1 and $2 \mathrm{~min}$ respectively. Equal weights of $A$ and $B$ are taken separately and allowed to disintegrate for $4 \mathrm{~min}$. What will be the ratio of weights of $A$ and $B$ disintegrated?
questions.solution:
For, $A t_{1 / 2}=1 \mathrm{~min}$
$\therefore$ part of $A$ remained after 4 minutes
$\text { (i.e., } 4 \text { half- life) will be }=\frac{1}{16}$
$\therefore$ part of $A$ disintegrated in the same period $=\frac{15}{16}$
For $B, t_{1 / 2}=2 \mathrm{~min}$
$\therefore$ Part of $B$ remained after 4 minutes (i.e., 2 half life)
$=\frac{1}{4}$
$\therefore$ part of $B$ disintegrated in the same time $=\frac{3}{4}$
Hence, ratio of disintegrated weights of $A$ and
$B=\frac{15}{16}: \frac{3}{4}=15: 12=5: 4$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: For a reversible reaction $\quad A \rightleftharpoons B$, which one of the following statements is wrong from the given energy profile diagram?
options: Activation energy of forward reaction is greater than backward reaction
The threshold energy is less than that of activation energy
The energy of activation of forward reaction is equal to the sum of heat of reaction and the energy of activation of backward reaction
questions.solution: where, $E_a=$ activation energy of forward reaction $E_a^{\prime}=$ activation energy of backward reaction The above energy profile diagram shows that $$ E_a>E_a^{\prime} $$ The potential energy of the product is greater than that of the reactant, so the reaction is endothermic. $$ \begin{aligned} & E_a=E_a^{\prime}+\Delta E \\ & E_t=E_a \text { or } E_t>E_a^{\prime} \end{aligned} $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: For a first order reaction at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the ratio of time required for $75 \%$ completion to $25 \%$ completion of reaction is
questions.solution:
For a first order reaction,
$t=\frac{2.303}{\lambda} \log _{10} \frac{a}{a-x}$
Let initial amount of reactant is 100 .
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{t_1}{t_2}= & \frac{\log \frac{100}{100-75}}{\log \frac{100}{100-25}} \\
& =\frac{\log \frac{100}{25}}{\log \frac{100}{75}}
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\log 4}{\log 4 / 3} \\ & =\frac{\log 4}{\log 4-\log 3} \\ & =\frac{2 \times 0.3010}{2 \times 0.3010-0.4771} \\ & =\frac{0.6020}{0.1249} \\ & =4.81\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
A certain reaction occurs in two steps as:

(i) 2SO2g+2NO2g→2SO3g+2NOg

(ii) 2NOg+O2g→2NO2g

In the reaction,
questions.solution:
It is a complex reaction because more then one steps are present.

On adding equation (1) and (2) we get the overall reaction.

2SO2+2NO2+O2→2SO3+2NO2
In overall reaction NO is not present.

Concept of intermediate is , it don't appear in overall reaction so NO is a intermediate in the reaction.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
A hypothetical reaction A2+B2→2AB follows the mechanism as given below:

A2A←→+A(fast)
A+B2→AB+B (slow)

A+B→AB (fast)

What will be the order of the overall reaction?
questions.solution:
r=kAB2

And kc=A[A][A2] or A=kc1/2A212

Thus,

r=k.kc12[A2]12[B2]

overall order is 32.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The energy of activation for an uncatalysed reaction is 100 kJ mol-1. Presence of a catalyst lowers the energy of activation by 75%. The log 1 0 K 2 K 1 of the ratio of rate constant of catalysed and uncatalysed reactions at 27°C is?

Assume the frequency factor is same for both reactions. (Given 2.303 × 8.314 = 19.147)
questions.solution:
In absence of catalyst, K 1 = Ae - 1 0 0 / RT

In presence of catalyst ; K 2 = Ae - 2 5 / RT

∴    K 2 K 1 = e 7 5 / RT ⇒ log e K 2 K 1 = 7 5 RT

⇒    log 1 0 K 2 K 1 = 7 5 2.303 × R × T

⇒    log 1 0 K 2 K 1 = 7 5 2.303 × 8.314 × 1 0 - 3 × 3 0 0 = 13.057
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The formation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> as per the reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>⟶</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced></math> is a second-order reaction, first order with respect to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and first order with respect to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are present in a closed vessel in ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math> maintained at a constant temperature with an initial total pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math>, what will be the total pressure in the vessel after the reaction is complete?
questions.solution:
In a closed container at a constant temperature

PV=nRT V & T constant

Pressure P∝n (Number of moles)  

The initial total pressure is 3 atm  and the partial pressures of NO2 and F2 will be in 2:1 ratio.              2NO2g   +    F2g  →  2NO2Fgt=0    2 atm             1 atmt=∞    -                   -                  2 atm              

Since the reaction is completed, so the total pressure after completion is due to NO2F only and it will be 2 atm.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The rate equation for a first order reaction is given by $[R]=[R]_0 e^{-k t}$. A straight line with positive slope is obtained by plotting. $[R]_0=$ initial concentration of reactant, $[R]=$ concentration of reactant at time, $t$
questions.solution:
For the equation $[R]=[R]_0 e^{-k t}$
Taking log we get
$$
\begin{array}{rlrl} 
& \ln [R] & =-k t+\operatorname{in}[R]_0 \\
& \therefore & \ln \frac{[R]}{[R]_0} & =-k t \\
& & & \\
k & =\frac{1}{t} \text { in } \frac{[R]_0}{[R]} \\
k & =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]}
\end{array}
$$
When graph is ploted of $\log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]}$ against $t$, we get a straight line with positive slope $=\frac{k}{2.303}$.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The decomposition of azomethane, at a certain temperature according to the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mfenced><msub><mtext>CH</mtext><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mo>N</mo><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>→</mo><msub><mtext>C</mtext><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mtext>H</mtext><mn>6</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mtext>N</mtext><mn>2</mn></msub></math>  is a first-order reaction. After <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> minutes from the start, the total pressure developed is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>350</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Hg</mi></math> in place of initial pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>of</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Hg</mi></math> of azomethane. The value of rate constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></math> is:
questions.solution:
This problem is based on conceptual mixing of determination of partial pressure and rate constant, which can be solved as follows.

k=2.303tlogPiPt

CH32N2→C2H6+N2

t=020000t=t200-xxx

Given,  200-x+x+x=350

                                          x = 1 5 0

              k = 2.303 t log 2 0 0 2 0 0 - 1 5 0

              k = 2.303 2 0 log 2 0 0 5 0

              k = 2.303 2 0 × 2 log 2

              k = 2.303 2 0 × 2 × 0.3010 = 0.693 1 0

                  = 6.93 × 1 0 - 2 min -1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

Which of the following potential energy Vs reaction co-ordinate diagram is consistent with given mechanism ?

E a 2    is highest other choices do not fulful this requirement.

Also esterification reaction Δ E ≈ 0   not much energy changes.

questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
If the rate constants of a reaction at 500 K and 700 K are 0.002 s-1 and 0.06 s-1, respectively, the value of activation energy is

R=8.314 J mol-1 K-1, log3=0.477
questions.solution:
We have logK2K1=Ea2.303RT2-T1T1T2

Putting the values, we get,

log0.060.002=Ea2.303×8.314700-500700×500

log30=Ea19.147200350000

1.477=Ea19.1472003500

Ea=49.49 kJ mol-1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which one of the following plots is correct for a first order reaction?
questions.solution: Integrated rate equation for first order reaction $$ k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{a}{(a-x)} $$ or $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{k}{2.303} t & =\log \frac{a}{(a-x)} \\ & =\log a-\log (a-x) \end{aligned} $$ or $\log (a-x)=-\frac{k}{2.303} t+\log a$ Thus, if $\log (a-x)$ values are plotted against time ' $t$ ' the graph obtained should be a straight line.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
For the reaction mechanism of the reaction 2 NO(g) + 2  H 2 (g)→ N 2 (g) + 2  H 2 O(g).

Step I 2 NO ⇌ k 1 N 2 O 2 ; K eq fast Step II N 2 O 2 +H 2 → k 2 N 2 O+H 2 O slow Step III N 2 O+H 2 → k 3 N 2 +H 2 O fast

Expression of rate of reaction is
(Take K eq  ×  k 2  = k')
questions.solution:
Step I 2 NO ⇌ k 1 N 2 O 2 ; K eq fast Step II N 2 O 2 + H 2 ⟶ k 2 N 2 O + H 2 O slow Step III N 2 O + H 2 ⟶ k 3 N 2 + H 2 O fast

(i)  Derive the rate Law

Rate =  k 2 N 2 O 2 H 2 (Rate determining step)

N 2 O 2 NO 2  =  K eq  ∴  N 2 O 2  =  K eq NO 2

Rate =  k 2   K eq NO 2 H 2

k' NO 2 H 2 Rate law is third order
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Consider the following gaseous decomposition of ozone:

2 O 3 g ⟶ 3 O 2 g .   The observed rate law is r = - d O 3 dt = k O 3 2 O 2
If the above reaction occurs through the following mechanism:

        O 3 ⇌ K O 2 + O fast equilibrium O + O 3 ⟶ k 1 2 O 2 slow

Then, which of the following relation is correct?
questions.solution:
In Complex Reaction Rate law is defined by the slowest step. So According to slowest step Rate Law Expression can be represented as:

Rate law=r=k1OO3...........(1)

Now Intermediate term ie [O] can never comes in Rate Law Expression.So to eliminate it,we apply Equilibrium constant Expression on Reversible Equation.

            K=O2OO3.............(2)    (K=Equilibrium constant)O=KO3O2

On Puting value of [O] in Equation 1, we get

   ⇒   r=k1KO32O2

  r=kO32O2    (k=k1K)
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The decomposition of $\mathrm{O}_3(g)$ follows first order kinetics and is given by
$$
\mathrm{O}_3(9) \longrightarrow \mathrm{O}_2(9)+\mathrm{O}(9)
$$
The rate constant for this reaction is $1.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The initial pressure of $\mathrm{O}_3(g)$ is $100 \mathrm{~atm}$.
What will be the partial pressure (in atm) of $\mathrm{O}_3, \mathrm{O}_2, \mathrm{O}$ respectively after 38.38 minutes?
questions.solution:
Decomposition of $\mathrm{O}_3$,
$$
\mathrm{O}_3(g) \longrightarrow \mathrm{O}_2(g)+\mathrm{O}(g)
$$
Initial pressure $100 \mathrm{~atm} \quad 0 \quad 0$
Partial pressure $100-x \quad x \quad x$ after $38.38 \mathrm{~m}$ in
$$
\begin{aligned}
p_i(\text { initial pressure }) & =100 \mathrm{~atm} \\
\left.p_f \text { (final pressure }\right) & \text { after } 38.38 \mathrm{~m} . \\
& =100-x
\end{aligned}
$$
From first order reaction,
Given, $\quad k=1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}, t=38.38 \mathrm{~m}$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
k & =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{p_i}{p_f} \\
1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} & =\frac{2.303}{38.38 \times 60} \log \frac{100(\mathrm{~atm})}{100-x(\mathrm{~atm})} \\
\log \frac{100}{100-x} & =\frac{1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \times 38.38 \times 60}{2.303}
\end{aligned}
$$


$$
x=90 \mathrm{~atm}
$$
Pressure of $\mathrm{O}_3$ after $38.38 \mathrm{~m}$.
$$
p \mathrm{o}_3=p_f=100-x=100-90 \mathrm{~atm}=10 \mathrm{~atm}
$$
Pressure of $\mathrm{O}_2(g)(x)=90 \mathrm{~atm}$
Pressure of $\mathrm{O}(g)(x)=90 \mathrm{~atm}$
Hence, option (3) is correct.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Consider the given figure and mark the correct option.
options: Activation energy of forward reaction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and product is less stable than reactant.

Activation energy of forward reaction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and product is more stable than reactant.

Activation energy of both forward and backward reaction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and reactant is more stable than product.
Activation energy of backward reaction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and product is more stable than reactant.
questions.solution: The excess energy given to the reactant molecules to raise their energy level to the level of threshold energy is called activation energy. From this graph, it can be observed that the activation energy of the forward reaction is E1+E2 and the product is less stable than the reactant due to high energy.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
For a decomposition of azoisopropane at 270°C it was found that at t = 0, the total pressure was 33.15 mm of Hg and after 3 minutes the total pressure was found to be 46.3 mm of Hg. Calculate the value of K for this reaction.
(CH3)2CHN=NCH(CH2)2→N2+C6H14
questions.solution:
k=2.303tlogpo(n-1)npo-p1
k=2.3033log33.15(2-1)2×33.15-46.3
k=0.168 min-1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A particular reaction has a rate constant $1.15 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. How long does it take for $6 \mathrm{~g}$ of the reactant of reduce to $3 \mathrm{~g}$ ? $(\log 2=0.301)$
questions.solution:
Given, rate constant $(k)=1.15 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
Reaction time $=$ ?
Initial amount of reactant $\left(A_o\right)=6 \mathrm{~g}$

Final amount of reactant $\left(A_f\right)=3 \mathrm{~g}$
From rate constant equation,
$$
\begin{aligned}
k & =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{A_o}{A_f} \\
1.15 \times 10^{-3} & =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{6}{3} \\
t & =\frac{2.303}{1.15 \times 10^{-3}} \log \frac{6}{3}=\frac{2.303}{1.15 \times 10^{-3}} \times .3010 \\
t & =.6027 \times 10^{+3} \mathrm{~s} \\
\text { or } \quad t & =603 \mathrm{~s}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: For a zero order reaction, the plot of concentration of reactant $v s$ time is (Hint: Consider the intercept on the concentration axis)
options: linear with +ve slope and non zero +ve intercept
linear with -ve slope and non zero + ve intercept
linear with -ve slope and zero intercept
linear with +ve slope and zero intercept
questions.solution:
Any reaction of zero order must obey the following equation $[A]=-k t+[A]_0 \text { or }[A]=[A]_0+k t$ This shows that, the concentration of reactant decreases linearly with time, as it is an equation of a straight line $(y=m x+c)$, the plot of $[A]$ versus $t$ will be straight line with slope $=-k$ and intercept on the concentration axis $=[A]_0$ as shown in figure.
Plot of $[A]$ versus $t$ for a reaction of zero order.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which one of the following reaction energy diagrams best represents a reaction in the forward direction; that is spontaneous/exothermic and complete in two consecutive steps in which the second step is little more difficult than the first step ?
questions.solution: Reaction in two consecutive steps imply two activated complexes corresponding to two peaks and one reaction intermediate corresponding to a minima in the valley of these activated complexes i.e., choice (i) and (ii) other are ruled out. Second step more difficult means activation energy for second step is higher confirmed.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The rate equation for a first-order reaction is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mfenced><mn>0</mn></msub><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>kt</mi></mrow></msup></math>. A straight line with positive slope is obtained by plotting (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mfenced><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo></math> Initial concentration of the reactant, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo></math> concentration of the reactant at time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math>)
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mfrac><msub><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mfenced><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mfenced></mfrac></math> Vs time

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mfrac><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mfenced><msub><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mfenced><mn>0</mn></msub></mfrac></math> Vs time
questions.solution:
First-order reaction is the rate of reaction is proportional to the first power of the concentration of the reactant R.

R→P

Rate =-dRdt=kR

Rate equation, R=R0e-kt

Comparing with y=mx+c, if we plot lnR against t, we get a straight line with a slope =-k and intercept =lnR0.

Also,

k=2.303tlogR0R⇒logR0R=kt2.303

If we plot a graph between logR0R Vs t, slope =k2.303.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The first order integrated rate equation is
questions.solution:
For first order,
$$
\text { rate }=\frac{d[R]}{d t}=k[R]
$$
or
$$
\frac{d[R]}{[R]}=k d t
$$
On integrating Eq. (i)
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\int \frac{d[R]}{[R]}=k \int d t \\
\ln [R]=-k t+C
\end{array}
$$
$\ldots$
$$
\text { At } t=0,[R]=\left[R_{0}\right]
$$
[where, $R=$ final concentration, ie, $a-x$ and
$R_{0}$ is the initial concentration, $\left.i e, a .\right]$
$$
\ln \left[R_{0}\right]=C
$$
On putting the value of $C$ in Eq. (ii), we get
$$
\begin{array}{r}
\ln [R]=-k t+\ln \left[R_{0}\right] \\
-k t=\ln [R]-\ln \left[R_{0}\right] \\
k t=\ln \left[R_{0}\right]-\ln [R]
\end{array}
$$
or
$$
\begin{aligned}
k &=\frac{1}{t} \ln \frac{\left[R_{0}\right]}{[R]} \\
or k&=\frac{1}{t} \ln \frac{a}{a-x}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which one of the following statements is correct?
options: In collision theory, \(e^{-E_{\mathrm{n}} / R T}\) corresponds to the fraction of molecules that have energy equal or greater than \(E_a\)
The number of collisions of reacting molecules per second per unit volume of the reaction mixture is activated complex
Molecularity is the number of molecules involved in a complex reaction
A catalyst catalyses the non-spontaneous reaction
questions.solution:
(a) In collision theory, \(e^{-E_a / R T}\) corresponds to the fraction of molecules that have energy equal to or greater than \(E_a\) (activation - energy) is the correct statement because it is in accordance with Arrhenius theory.
(b) The number of collision of the reacting molecules per second per unit volume of the reaction mixture is activated complex is not a correct statement as activated complexes are formed during the reaction, which finally gives the product.
(c) Molecularity is the number of molecules involved in a complex reaction. This is also not correct as molecularity is defined as: The number of reacting species taking part in a reaction, which must collide simultancously in order to bring out the reaction.
(d) A catalyst, catalyses the non-spontaneous reaction. This is also not correct as a catalyst can catalyse the spontaneous as well as non-spontaneous reactions.
Hence, option (a) is the correct answer.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: For the following reaction of first-order <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, if the total pressure of the system after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> minutes is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and after a very long time, total pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> for the reaction is
questions.solution:
After a very long time, the reaction can be assumed to be completed.

Thus, for the given reaction,

N2O5→2NO2+12O2t=0P000t=20 minsP0-p2pp2t→∞02P0P02

According to the question, after a very long time,

2P0+P02=400⇒52P0=400⇒P0=160 mm

And, after 20 minutes,

P0-p+2p+p2=220 mm⇒160+32p=220⇒32p=60⇒p=40 mm

Now, we know that, k=0.693t1/2

Applying the integrated rate law after 20 mins of reactions,

20=2.303klog160160-40⇒20=2.303×t1/20.693 log 160120⇒20×0.6932.303=t1/2×log 43⇒t1/2≈ 6log 43
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Which of the following curves represents a zero order reaction?

[a-x=reactant concentration]
questions.solution:
For the zero order reaction,

At=A0-kt

and x=kt where, x=A0-At.

So, graph between concentration and time will be straight line with positive slope.

Graph between x and t will be straight line passing through origin.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
For a reaction $A+B \longrightarrow P$, the following data are provided
The rate constant for this reaction in standard unit is
questions.solution:
(d) $A+B \longrightarrow P$
According to rate law expression
$$
\text { Rate }=k[A]^\alpha[B]^\beta(K \text { is constant }) \ldots (i)
$$
Taking ratio of (1) and (2) entry,
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-2}} & =\frac{[0.04]^\beta[0.02]^\alpha}{[0.02]^\beta[0.02]^\alpha} \\
2 & =[2]^\beta \\
{[2]^1 } & =[2]^\beta \\
\beta & =1
\end{aligned}
$$
Taking ratio of (2) and (3) entry,
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{8 \times 10^{-2}}{4 \times 10^{-2}} & =\frac{[0.04]^\alpha[0.04]^\beta}{[0.02]^\alpha[0.04]^\beta} \\
{[2] } & =[2]^\alpha \Rightarrow \alpha=1
\end{aligned}
$$
Put value of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in (1) entry
$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \times 10^{-2} & =k[A]^1[B]^1 \\
k & =\frac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{0.02 \times 0.02} \\
& =\frac{2 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^4}{4}=\frac{100}{2}=50
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Why do we use the carbon dating to calculate the age of the fossil?
options: Rate of exchange of carbon between atmosphere and living is slower than decay of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>C</mi><mn>14</mn></msup></math>.
It is not appropriate to use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math>dating to determine age.

Rate of exchange of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math>between atmosphere and living organism is so fast that an equilibrium is set up between the intake of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>C</mi><mrow><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math>by organism and its exponential decay.
questions.solution:
It is clear from the 3rd paragraph, which states that in living organisms a dynamic equilibrium is established where by the ratio of C14 to C12 remains constant.

Hence, C14 which decays into N14 is replenished by the production of new isotopes.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The half-life periods of a first order reaction at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ and $400 \mathrm{~K}$ are $50 \mathrm{~s}$ and $10 \mathrm{~s}$ respectively. The activation energy of the reaction in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ is $(\log 5=0.70)$
questions.solution:
Given,

For first order reaction,
(i) half-life $\left(t_{1 / 2}\right)$ at temperature $300 \mathrm{~K}=50 \mathrm{~s}$
(ii) half-life $\left(t_{1 / 2}\right)$ at temperature $400 \mathrm{~K}=10 \mathrm{~s}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad K_1(\text { at } 300 \mathrm{~K})=\frac{0.693}{50}=0.014 \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\
& \text { and } K_2(\text { at } 400 \mathrm{~K})=\frac{0.693}{10}=0.07 \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\
& \left.\qquad \because K=0.693 / t_{(1 / 2)} \text { for lst order reaction }\right]
\end{aligned}
$$
where, $K_1$ and $K_2$ are rate constant at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ and $400 \mathrm{~K}$ respectively.
Also, according to Arrhenius theory
$$
\log \frac{K_2}{K_1}=\frac{E_a}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{T_2-T_1}{T_1 \cdot T_2}\right]
$$
where, $E_a=$ activation energy
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 & =\text { temperature }(300 \mathrm{~K}) \\
T_2 & =\text { temperature }(400 \mathrm{~K}) \\
R & =\operatorname{gas} \text { constant }\left(8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \log \frac{0.07}{0.014}=\frac{E_a}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{400-300}{400 \times 300}\right] \\
& \text { or, } \quad \log 5=\frac{E_a}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\frac{100}{400 \times 300}\right] \\
& \text { or, } \quad 0.70=\frac{E_a}{19.15 \times 1200} \\
& \text { or, } \quad E_a=0.7 \times 19.15 \times 1200 \\
& =16086 \mathrm{~J} \\
& =16.08 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& E_a \approx 16.10 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, option (c) is the correct answer.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: For reaction $2 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{B} \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}+\mathrm{E}$, following mechanism has been proposed $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}$ (Is slow) and $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{E}$ (Is fast).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The half-life for decay of radioactive   14 C  is 5730 years. An archaeological artefact containing wood has only 80% of the   14 C  activity as found in living trees. The age of the artefact would be:
[Given: log 1.25 = 0.0969]
questions.solution:
Radioactive decay follows first order kinetics.   Let  [A] 0 =100

  ∴ [A]=100×80%=80 Decay constant ​(k)= 0.693 t 1/2 = 0.693 5730 t= 2.303 k  log [Ao] [A] = 2.303 0.693 5730  log 100 80 = 2.303×5730 0.693 × log1.25 = 2.303×5730 0.693 ×0.0969 = 1 8 4 5  yrs
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Rate of reaction for $2 \mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~W}+\mathrm{Z}$ is $1.2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}$ when $[\mathrm{X}]=[\mathrm{Y}]=0.6 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$
Calculate value of rate constant if reaction is first order in $\mathrm{X}$ and zero order in Y
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{R}=-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{X}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{Y}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{1}{3} \frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{W}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{Z}]}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{X}] \\ & 1.2 \times 10^{-4}=\mathrm{k}(0.6) \\ & \mathrm{k}=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Two reactions, <math> <mrow> <mtext>A</mtext> <mo>→</mo> <mtext>Products</mtext> <mtext> and</mtext> <mtext> B</mtext> <mo>→</mo> <mtext>Products</mtext> </mrow> </math>, have rate constants K<sub>a</sub> and K<sub>b</sub> at temperature T and activation energies E<sub>a</sub> and E<sub>b</sub> respectively. If K<sub>a</sub> > K<sub>b</sub> and E<sub>a</sub> < E<sub>b</sub> and assuming that A for both the reactions is same then :
options: At higher temperatures K<sub>a</sub> will be greater than K<sub>b</sub>
At lower temperature K<sub>a</sub> and K<sub>b</sub> will different more and K<sub>a</sub> > K<sub>b</sub>
As temperature rises K<sub>a</sub> and K<sub>b</sub> will be close to each other in magnitude
questions.solution:
​​(I) As temperature Rises

Ka will increase Kb will also increase  

  since Ea < Eb 
           Ka >> Kb

(II)  At lower temperature 

Ka will fall

Kb will fall

But as Ea < Eb

           K'a > K'b 

(II)  As temperature rises very high Ka and Kb will try to close each other as they are exponential functions of temperature
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of the following equations represents integrated rate law for zero order reaction?
questions.solution:
Let us consider a zero order reaction,
$$ \begin{aligned} & -\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{A}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^0 \\ & -\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{A}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^0=\mathrm{k} \\ & -\int \mathrm{d}[\mathrm{A}]=\int \mathrm{kdt} \\ & -[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}=\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \text { at } \mathrm{t}=0 \quad \quad[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}=[\mathrm{A}]_0 \\ & -[\mathrm{A}]_0=\mathrm{k} \times 0+\mathrm{C} \\ & \mathrm{C}=-[\mathrm{A}]_0 \end{aligned} $$ On substituting the value of $C$ $$ \begin{aligned} & -[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}=\mathrm{kt}-[\mathrm{A}]_0 \\ & {[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}=[\mathrm{A}]_0-\mathrm{kt}} \\ & \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{mx} \\ & {[\mathrm{A}]_0-[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}=\mathrm{kt}} \\ & \mathrm{k}=\frac{[\mathrm{A}]_0-[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}{\mathrm{t}} \end{aligned} $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The rate constant, $\mathrm{k}$ for a first order reaction, $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{I}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{HI}(\mathrm{g})$ is $\mathrm{xs}^{-1}$ at $600 \mathrm{~K}$ and $4 \mathrm{x} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ at $700 \mathrm{~K}$.
The energy of activation of the reaction (in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) is
questions.solution:
$\log \frac{\mathrm{K}_2}{\mathrm{~K}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_1 \mathrm{~T}_2}\right)$
$\begin{aligned} & \log \left(\frac{4 \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303(8.314)}\left(\frac{700-600}{700 \times 600}\right) \\ & 0.602=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{19.147}\left(2.38 \times 10^{-4}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=48548.3 \mathrm{~J}=48.54 \mathrm{~kJ} \\ & \approx 48.16 \mathrm{~kJ} .\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: For an elementary reaction, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></msub><mo>⟶</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></msub></math>, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> minutes. In what period of time would the concentration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> be reduced to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of its original concentration?
questions.solution:
X(g)⟶Y(g)+Z(g)

Let the concentration at t=0 = x

The concentration after time t=t = a-x=10%x = 0.1x

K=0.693t12=0.69310=0.0693

According to the integrated rate law,

t=1Klnaa-x=10.0693ln10.1=10.0693ln10=2.3030.0693=33.2

Hence, the time is equal to the 33.2 minutes
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
In the sequence of reaction,

A→     k1     B→     k2     C→     k3     D, where k3>k2>k1, then the rate-determining step of the reaction is
questions.solution:
In a chemical reaction, slowest step will be the rate-determining step.

A→    k1    B→    k2    C→    k3    D

∵k3>k2>k1

k1 is minimum out of the given rate constants. So the slowest step is A→B, which will be the rate determining step of the reaction.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
A substance undergoes a first-order decomposition. The decomposition follows two parallel first-order reactions as given below.

The percentage distributions of B and C are

(k1=1.26×10-4 s-1 and k2=3.8×10-5 s-1)
questions.solution:
In the given parallel reaction, a reactant A is dissociating to produce two products B and C simultaneously as follows,

Where k1=1.26×10-4s-1 and k2=3.8×10-5s-1

In a parallel reaction, the ratio of the concentrations of products at any time is equal to the ratio of the rate constant of their respective reactions, i.e.,

BC=k1k2

Then, the percentage of B can be calculated as follows,

%B=k1k1+k2×100%B=1.26×10-41.26×10-4+3.8×10-5×100%B=76.83 %

Similarly, the percentage of C,

%C=k2k1+k2×100%C=3.8×10-51.26×10-4+3.8×10-5×100%C=23.17%

Therefore, the percentage of B and C will be 76.83 % and 23.17 %, respectively.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
For an exothermic reaction, following two steps are involved.

Step 1. A + B → I slow Step 2. I  → AB fast

Which of the following graphs correctly represent this reaction ?
questions.solution: In any reaction mechanism the no. of activated complexes showing maxima of potential energies is the no. of steps in that mechanism and in between these maxima is a valley where lies a more stable intermediate. There is one reaction intermediate lying at valley of two maxima representing two steps. E<sub>a</sub> for first step should be higher than second step as first is slow step. These requirements fulfilled by choice (ii) & not choice (i) the choice (iv) is not possible because that is for endothermic reaction <math> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mo>Δ</mo> <mtext>H</mtext> <mo>+</mo> <mtext>ve</mtext> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math>& (ii) is single step equation so not possible.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Rate of reaction $\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y} \rightarrow$ product is $5.4 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}$. When $[\mathrm{x}]=0.2 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$ and $[\mathrm{y}]=0.1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$, calculate rate constant of reaction if it is first order in $\mathrm{X}$ and second order in $\mathrm{Y}$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{r}=-\frac{\mathrm{d}(\mathrm{x})}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{\mathrm{d}(\mathrm{y})}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \mathrm{r}=5.4 \times 10^{-2} \frac{\mathrm{mol}}{\mathrm{dm}^3-\mathrm{sec}} \\ & \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{x}] \cdot[\mathrm{y}]^2 \\ & 5.4 \times 10^{-2}=\mathrm{K}(0.2)(0.1)^2 \\ & \mathrm{~K}=27 \mathrm{~mol}^{-2} \mathrm{dm}^6 \mathrm{sec}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
For a reaction
$\mathrm{NO}_2+\mathrm{CO} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2+\mathrm{NO}$ the possible elementary steps (below $500 \mathrm{~K}$ ) are
Step $1: \mathrm{NO}_2+\mathrm{NO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text { Slow }} \mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{NO}_3$ 
Step $2: \mathrm{NO}_3+\mathrm{CO} \xrightarrow{\text { Fast }} \mathrm{CO}_2+\mathrm{NO}_2$ 
It's rate expression will be
questions.solution:
For a reaction
$\begin{aligned} & m X+n Y \longrightarrow Z \\ & \text { rate }=k[X]^m[Y]^n\end{aligned}$
Rate of reaction is the slower step of the reaction.
$\mathrm{NO}_2+\mathrm{NO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text { Slow }} \mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{NO}_3$
$\therefore \quad$ Rate $=k\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]^2$.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
